message:
fix(fp-io): tighten autoincrement detection for composite PKs (br-3i43 / fd90.42)

Self-review of fd90.37 surfaced a documented false positive: the
per-row heuristic 'pk == 1 && type == INTEGER' incorrectly marked
the first INTEGER column of a composite PK as autoincrement. SQLite's
rowid-alias rule is more specific: it requires the column to be the
SOLE primary key.

Fix: refactor the rusqlite table_schema impl from a single-pass loop
to a two-pass design. First pass collects all PRAGMA table_info rows
into a Vec<ColumnInfoRow> alias and counts how many have pk > 0.
Second pass builds SqlColumnSchema entries, marking autoincrement
only when pk_count == 1 AND the column has pk == 1 AND its
declared_type is INTEGER (case-insensitive).

Update the existing
sql_table_schema_autoincrement_not_set_for_composite_pk_integer
test (which explicitly documented the false positive as
'captures actual behavior, not idealized rule') to verify the fix:
all three composite-PK columns must now have autoincrement=false
while keeping their declaration-order PK ordinals.

Add a new sql_table_schema_autoincrement_two_pass_count_distinguishes
_single_vs_composite test demonstrating the contrast: single
INTEGER PRIMARY KEY -> autoincrement=true, composite (a INTEGER,
b INTEGER) PRIMARY KEY -> autoincrement=false on both columns even
though both have INTEGER type and pk > 0.

Type alias ColumnInfoRow added inside the function body to keep
clippy::type_complexity happy on the intermediate Vec.

410 fp-io tests pass; clippy -D warnings clean across fp-io +
umbrella; ubs Critical=0.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -81,6 +81,7 @@
 {"id":"frankenpandas-3d5q","title":"[HIGH] provenance: no commit-signing policy or CI signature verification — supply-chain attestation missing before crates.io release","description":"Commit provenance + signing audit.

**Observation.** grep for `gpgsign|signed|ssh` in AGENTS.md returns nothing. grep for `commit.gpgsign` in the repo returns nothing. The Skill system prompt warned me explicitly: "NEVER skip hooks (--no-gpg-sign) unless user requests" — implying signing MAY be configured — but nothing in the repo requires it.

Verified via `git log --show-signature -5`: no SIGNED entries. Recent commits include both me (cc-pandas via `git -c user.name="cc-pandas"`) and the upstream Dicklesworthstone identity. Neither is cryptographically attested.

**Context — why this matters for a public library:**
- crates.io accepts publishes from anyone with API credentials + a matching Cargo.toml author. It doesn't verify commit signatures.
- docs.rs builds whatever source the tag points at. No signature check.
- The multi-agent swarm has several authorial identities mingling in `git log`. Auditing "did a real human approve this commit?" requires either (a) trusting `Author:` fields (which anyone can spoof) or (b) requiring signed commits.
- When the PyO3 binding ships (br-diic) + the release workflow lands (br-4clx), frankenpandas becomes a supply-chain dependency for downstream projects. Users of the Python wheel have no way to verify the tag that built it came from a trusted identity.
- Even within the swarm: if one compromised agent identity starts committing malicious code, reverting requires distinguishing legit commits from attack commits.

**Severity.** HIGH — supply-chain provenance for a library that will publish to crates.io. Low overhead to add; high cost to retrofit after a provenance incident.

**Fix shape:**

1. Documented policy in AGENTS.md + CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s):
   - All human contributors configure SSH commit signing (simpler than GPG, GitHub supports it since 2022):
       git config commit.gpgsign true
       git config gpg.format ssh
       git config user.signingkey ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
   - Swarm-agent identities publish their signing keys in a committed `AUTHORS.md` with public-key fingerprints. Identity-to-key mapping becomes auditable.
   - AGENTS.md gets a section: "How to prove your agent commits are yours, not spoofed."

2. CI gate:
   - ci.yml adds `- name: Verify commit signatures` running `git log --format='%G?' -1 <commit> | grep -q '^[GU]'` (G = good signature, U = good with unknown key).
   - Or: enforce via GitHub branch protection rule (toggle in repo settings) — requires signed commits to merge into main.

3. Release provenance:
   - br-4clx (release workflow) — tag signing: `git tag -s vX.Y.Z` with the release manager's key. crates.io publish reads the signed tag via the release-plz tooling; SLSA-level attestation becomes possible.
   - Consider Sigstore/cosign for supply-chain attestation alongside GPG signing — gives us a verifiable chain from source → tag → published crate.

4. Swarm agent identities:
   - Each agent that commits (cc-pandas, cod, etc.) has its own ssh signing key, committed public key in AUTHORS.md.
   - Commits tagged `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>` get the Co-Authored-By line verified via the primary author's signature chain.

Blast radius: AGENTS.md addition (+~30 lines), AUTHORS.md (new, ~20 lines for current identities), ci.yml (+1 step ~10 lines), repo settings (branch protection toggle — requires repo admin). Zero source code changes.

Related:
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — policy docs live here.
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — private disclosure channel; the corresponding public provenance is addressed by this bead.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — tag signing integrated with the publish pipeline.
- br-36qc (cargo-audit) + br-hg60 (secret scan) — the same supply-chain hygiene family.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:41:17.728743310Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:12:31.798396759Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T17:12:31.795924057Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["provenance","review","security","supply-chain"]}

+{"id":"frankenpandas-3i43","title":"fd90.42: tighten autoincrement detection for composite PKs (defect from fd90.37)","description":"fd90.37's autoincrement detection has a documented false positive: composite PK where the first column is INTEGER gets autoincrement=true even though SQLite's rowid-alias rule requires the column to be the SOLE primary key. Fix: tighten the heuristic from per-row 'pk==1 && type==INTEGER' to a two-pass check: first count columns with pk>0; only mark a column as autoincrement when it has pk==1 AND it's the only one with pk>0 AND its declared_type is INTEGER. The existing test sql_table_schema_autoincrement_not_set_for_composite_pk_integer documented the false positive — flip its assertion to verify the correct behavior. Add a confirming test that single-PK INTEGER and composite-PK with INTEGER first behave correctly. No struct API change — pure heuristic refinement inside the rusqlite override.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas-2","created_at":"2026-04-25T22:20:11.273173836Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T22:23:33.091794135Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T22:23:33.089026646Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fd90","pandas-parity","review","sql"]}
